summary.en: The historic and cultural center of Scranton, where grand Gilded Age architecture stands beside modern offices. The energy is a mix of civic pride, arts, and the legacy of the railroad barons.
A stately, largely residential district defined by its historic mansions, quiet tree-lined streets, and intellectual atmosphere. A walk here feels like stepping back into the city's prosperous past.

Raw, authentic, and deeply historic, this area is a tangible connection to the iron, coal, and steam that built the city. It offers a powerful look at the industrial ruins and relics that are the city's true foundation.

This area offers a journey into the anthracite coal mining industry that defined life for generations. It’s a mix of community parks and unique cultural sites providing a deeper understanding of the city's labor legacy.

name.en: Nay Aug Park & The East Side

summary.en: Scranton's green lung, dominated by the magnificent Nay Aug Park. It’s a place for leisurely walks, cultural exploration, and quiet contemplation, blending natural beauty with impressive institutions.
A modern recreational hub on the city's southern edge, this is Scranton's playground. It’s a place of sweeping views, outdoor activity, and large-scale entertainment.

description.en: Once the palatial DL&W Railroad station, its lobby features a breathtaking Tiffany stained-glass ceiling and marble walls, acting as a living museum of the steam-railroad era.

name.en: Scranton Cultural Center at the Masonic Temple

description.en: An architectural marvel in neo-Gothic and Romanesque style, this imposing building is a hub for the city's performing arts.
A semi-permanent piece of public art celebrating the fictional paper company from 'The Office', making it a quirky, meta-landmark for fans.
The seat of the Polish National Catholic Church, a unique denomination founded in Scranton. Its twin spires are a neighborhood landmark.

name.en: Lackawanna Historical Society at the Catlin House

description.en: Housed in a beautifully preserved 1912 mansion, this is the essential stop for understanding the region's deep history, with the house itself being a primary exhibit.
A National Historic Landmark and former home of one of America's most influential 19th-century labor leaders. A humble monument to working-class history.
Wander down streets like Monroe, Clay, and Taylor Avenues to see a stunning variety of Victorian, Tudor, and Colonial Revival homes built by industrial magnates.
Four massive, stone blast furnace stacks that are the silent, monumental ruins of the industry that founded the city. An open-air museum and moody photo location.
An immersive museum complex dedicated to steam railroading, featuring a staggering collection of colossal locomotives in a massive roundhouse and rail yard.
Located next to Steamtown, this museum preserves and operates historic streetcars, offering a different perspective on local transit and scenic excursion rides.
A hidden gem of modern lore, this oversized train set was donated to the Trolley Museum by the host of HBO's 'Last Week Tonight' after a local news segment went viral.
The quintessential Scranton experience, descending 300 feet underground in a mine car for an unforgettable look at the conditions miners endured.
A sprawling park that houses the mine tour, offering expansive green space, walking trails, and a rugged feel.
A wonderfully eccentric and beloved local institution, this museum is a passionate tribute to the world's most famous magician, complete with a live magic show.

name.en: Everhart Museum of Natural History, Science & Art

description.en: A classic 'cabinet of curiosities' museum nestled inside the park, with an eclectic collection ranging from dinosaur fossils to fine art.
The main attraction of the area, designed by Frederick Law Olmsted, with walking trails that reveal curated views and create a sense of escape from the city.

name.en: Basilica of the National Shrine of St. Ann

description.en: A stunning Romanesque church and major pilgrimage site. Its impressive domes are a landmark, and the interior is equally grand and awe-inspiring.
A hub for winter sports that offers the single best panoramic viewpoint of the Lackawanna Valley from its summit, accessible by chairlift or hiking in the off-season.
A massive, 17,500-seat amphitheater built into the mountainside that hosts major national concerts and events.
Home to the Scranton/Wilkes-Barre RailRiders, the Triple-A affiliate of the New York Yankees. Catching a minor league game here is a classic American experience.

prompt.en: Find the most impressive Gilded Age building you can and take a photo from a low angle to make it look even grander.
Capture a photo that shows the mix of old and new architecture in a single frame.
Find a subtle (or not-so-subtle) reference to 'The Office' somewhere in the neighborhood and snap a picture.
Take a selfie in front of a building that looks important, like a government office or a grand bank.
Find an interesting carving, gargoyle, or piece of stonework on an old building and take a close-up shot.
Imagine you were a wealthy railroad baron. Take a photo of a view that you would find impressive.
Capture the reflection of a historic building in the window of a modern one.
Take a photo looking straight down a street, emphasizing the grid layout of the downtown core.
Find a sign, poster, or building related to the performing arts and take a creative photo.
Take a close-up photo of an interesting texture you find on a wall, sidewalk, or door.
Find a scene where three distinct colors stand out and frame them in a photo.
Photograph an old, vintage-looking sign on a storefront or building.
Capture a candid moment of daily life happening on one of the main avenues.
Take a picture of an interesting shadow cast by one of the grand downtown buildings.
Take a selfie with a clock, statue, or fountain that looks like a local landmark.
Find the most impressive historic mansion and take a photo of it. Which one would you live in?
Capture the quiet, peaceful feeling of a tree-lined street in this neighborhood.
Photograph the most interesting or inviting front porch you can find.
Find an architectural detail that looks like the first letter of your name and take a picture.
Take a selfie on a quiet street, with a grand home in the background.
Find a house with ornate Victorian trim or 'gingerbread' details and take a close-up photo.
Photograph a beautifully manicured garden or yard in front of one of the historic homes.
Find a house with a unique or unusual window (stained glass, circular, etc.) and make it the focus of your photo.
Find a quiet spot to sit, like a bench or a stoop, and take a photo of your view.
Locate a house with Tudor-style half-timbering on its exterior and snap a picture.
Look for a historical plaque or marker on a building or in a yard and photograph it.
Take a photo that showcases the different textures of brick and stone used in the neighborhood's architecture.
Find the most impressive doorway or entrance to a home and capture it in a photo.
Look up! Take a photo focusing on the interesting rooflines, chimneys, and gables of the houses.
Take a photo that includes both a historic home and a modern car, showing the passage of time.
Find a remnant of the industrial era—a brick smokestack, old factory wall, or railroad track—and take a powerful photo.
Take a close-up, abstract photo of a rusty metal surface or weathered industrial brick.
Capture a photo that evokes the age of steam, iron, and coal. Think dramatic angles and gritty textures.
Take a selfie with a large piece of old machinery, an industrial ruin, or a historic train.
Take a photo that emphasizes the massive scale of an industrial structure, like a furnace, train, or factory building.
Photograph something that looks like it was part of the city's original foundation - old stone, brickwork, or steel.
Capture the raw, authentic, and less-polished vibe of the South Side in a single image.
Find evidence of Scranton's transportation history (trolley tracks, railroad lines, old bridges) and photograph it.
Find something quirky or modern that feels like a new piece of local lore and snap a pic.
Frame a shot with an industrial silhouette (smokestack, furnace) against the sky.
Take a photo that conveys a sense of immense power, inspired by the industrial machinery of the area.
Find a spot where nature (vines, grass, trees) is growing on or around an old industrial structure.
Try to take a photo from the perspective of a 19th-century iron or railroad worker.
Capture a long, dramatic shadow cast by one of the area's historic industrial relics.
Find a detail that most people would walk past, but that tells a story about the area's industrial history.
Take a photo that hints at the coal mining history buried beneath the streets. Look for signs, memorials, or related structures.
Capture a photo of people enjoying the green space at a local park.
Find something wonderfully weird or eccentric in the neighborhood and take a picture of it.
Take a selfie that honors the working-class history of the area.
Photograph a place that looks like a gathering spot for the local community.
Inspired by the Houdini Museum, take a photo that has a magical or mysterious quality.
Find a spot in a park that feels more rugged and less manicured and capture its natural beauty.
Photograph something you were completely surprised to find in this neighborhood.
Take a photo that seems to represent the long history of families and community in the area.
Take a photo of an interesting walking trail, path, or sidewalk in the neighborhood.
Inspired by the coal mine, take a photo that plays with strong contrast between light and shadow.
Find a building or home that has a unique and quirky character and photograph it.
Take a photo looking up at the sky from a low angle, imagining what it was like to emerge from the mine.
Find a spot that feels like a hidden gem and take a selfie there.
Find a sign (old or new) that tells a story about the neighborhood's culture or history.
Find a spot in the park where you can't see any buildings and take a photo that captures the feeling of a natural escape.
Take a photo of a path or trail that curves gracefully through the landscape, reflecting Olmsted's design philosophy.
Find a strange or beautiful natural object (an unusual leaf, a weird rock, a cool tree bark) and take a close-up photo.
Take a selfie with a backdrop of lush greenery in the park.
Find something that inspires a sense of awe, whether it's a grand building or a beautiful natural view, and capture it.
Photograph a scene that represents leisure and relaxation in the park.
Use tree branches, leaves, or other natural elements to frame a photo of a distant object or view.
Take a photo of one of the impressive institutional buildings (museum, church) in or near the park.
Find a peaceful spot and take a photo that makes you feel calm just by looking at it.
Look for art in the neighborhood, whether it's a statue, a mural, or the architecture of a museum.
Find the most vibrant patch of color in the park—flowers in spring, green leaves in summer, autumn foliage—and make it your subject.
Walk along a path until a beautiful view is revealed. Capture that specific, curated view.
Find a source of water in the park—a creek, a pond, a fountain—and take a reflective photo.
Lie down (or just look up) and take a photo of the sky as seen through the canopy of trees.
Find a quiet bench and take a photo of the view from it, capturing a moment of quiet contemplation.
Get to a high point and take the most sweeping panoramic photo of the valley you can.
Take a photo that captures the feeling of outdoor activity and recreation, whether it's sports, hiking, or entertainment.
Capture an image that shows the contrast between the modern recreational facilities and the natural mountainside.
Take a selfie from a high vantage point with the valley sprawling out behind you.
Photograph something that represents the 'playground' feel of the area—a ski lift, a baseball field, a concert stage.
Take a photo of one of the large-scale entertainment venues and try to capture its impressive size.
Find a hiking trail or path and take a photo looking up or down the mountain.
Find a scene that feels like a classic American experience, inspired by the minor league baseball field.
Take a photo from the base of the mountain looking up, emphasizing its height.
Find an element of modern architecture or design and photograph it against the natural backdrop.
Capture a photo that gives a sense of wide open space and big sky.
Take a photo that represents the current season on the mountain (skis/snow in winter, green hills in summer).
Photograph the winding road that leads up the mountain.
From a viewpoint, take a photo focusing on the historic city core in the distance.
Take a photo of a sign or piece of equipment (like a chairlift) that suggests an adventure is about to begin.
